text: Now, you don't need me to tell you that it's been a somewhat underwhelming year for Hollywood movies, but screw it. I'm going to do it anyway. So, indulge me for a moment while I get this off my chest. Dear Hollywood, you in fact you with a [ __ ] anchor, including the chain, you bunch of talentless, brain dead, cowardly. In any sane world, you wouldn't even be in charge of a [ __ ] toll booth. Never mind a multi-billion dollar industry that reshapes the face of Western

culture. Your remakes and your superhero movies and your modern audience. In fact, if I had my way, I would shove a [ __ ] mobile phone right up every single one of your big [ __ ] hairy sideways, mind you, and keep calling it all day long until you all get [ __ ] rectile cancer. Kind regards, the critical drinker. Right now that's out of the way, it's time to cast our eyes forward to the far-flung future of March 2026 when Project Hail Mary is going to get released. Now, if there's any movie

that can stir something vaguely resembling excitement in the cold ashes of my black heart, it's probably this. Yeah, maybe that's because I'm a big fan of the book, which was written by the same author as The Martian, and that got adapted into probably the last good film we'll ever see from Ridley Scott. Or maybe it's because I'm an optimist at heart and I desperately want to see something succeed that isn't a remake, a brain deadad franchise sequel, or yet

another tedious cape [ __ ] disaster. Or maybe it's because they just released a new trailer for it. And I'm not going to lie, it looks pretty [ __ ] good. >> No way. Yes way. So, rather than make another video bemoning the state of modern cinema, I thought it might be a refreshing change to look at something I'm actually optimistic about and explain why I want it to do well. Now, for anyone who's not familiar with the source material, allow me to furnish you

with some background. The story set in the near future where scientists discover that our sun seems to be getting mysteriously dimmer, almost like it's running out of energy, which is a bit concerning really because it's not supposed to happen for a few billion years yet. The world's best minds get to work on the problem and eventually discover that it's been infested by a microscopic organism known as astroofage that migrates from star to star and literally feeds on solar energy. And

because they can store enormous amounts of it inside their own bodies, they can move almost at the speed of light, which allows them to infest all the stars in our neighborhoods. Naturally, this is bad news for planet Earth because eventually the astrophase will consume all of the sun's fuel and the human race will die out. Something has to be done and fast. That's when one particular star gets discovered that doesn't seem to have been affected like its neighbors. Clearly, there's something

about it that the Astrophage don't like. So, we need to find out what it is. Thus, a plan is formed. Use the Astrophage as a fuel source to propel a ship at close to light speed to this nearby star. Find out what the hell seems to be keeping them away from it, and bring it back to Earth before the sun goes out and everyone dies. No pressure then. And because it's kind of a long shot with slim chances of success, it soon gets dubbed Project Hail Mary. A team is assembled to head

up the mission, including Dr. Ryland Grace, the molecular biologist who first figured out how the astrophase works. I mean, he's not exactly thrilled to be on the mission, but when the fate of your entire planet's at stake, you probably have to say yes. But when he wakes up from cryosleep at the destination, he discovers that the rest of the crew are dead, and he's got no memory of who he is or why he's there. Well, that's not a great start, is it? [ __ ] gets even more

real when the Hail Mary encounters an alien spacecraft that turns out to be on the same mission as his. Turns out the Earth isn't the only planet looking for a solution to the astrophase problem. But are they going to attack him? Or can he somehow learn to communicate with the ship's only crew member and work together to help save both of their planets? It's a neat little premise that plays around with the insane physics behind near light speeded travel that

introduces a bunch of fascinating sci-fi concepts in a way that even a layman can understand and gives us an alien species that feels genuinely alien for once instead of just a slightly ugly humanoid in a rubber suit. And yeah, I'm looking at you, Predator Badlands. But at its core, Project Hail Mary is really a story about friendship and loyalty in the most unusual circumstances. two wildly different individuals learning to work together to overcome their differences because the fate of both

their worlds depends on them and there's literally no one else. And ultimately what they're willing to risk to help each other out when everything starts to go wrong and it seems like there's no hope left. I say all of this because I want you to understand what makes the book so compelling so you can also understand what I'm hoping to see translated into the movie. And in terms of the visuals and the production design, they seem to have nailed the look of the novel perfectly. Just like

The Martian, the technology and design of the Hail Mary looks grounded and believable, like it was cobbled together in a rush from existing spacecraft parts, which it was. The alien ship looks exactly like I pictured it, and so does Rocky, its pilot. It's kind of a long story, but his species evolved in an extremely harsh environment, so he basically resembles a giant crab made out of rock. He doesn't have a face like we'd understand it, or even a head for that matter. So, the big challenge

they're going to have with this one is making him emote enough that the audience can empathize with him. Ryan Goslin was a solid choice for Ryland. He's already proven he can balance humor with drama and believably play everyman type characters with a bit of likability, so that's no big issue. My only slight concern though is the choice of director or directors in this case. Phil Lord and Chris Miller obviously have a natural instinct for comedy and when they're at their best they can

produce great stuff but they haven't successfully directed a movie together since 2014 and that movie was 22 Jump Street. >> My name is Jeff. >> Project Hail Mary as a book definitely has plenty of levity in it and Ryland himself does a bit of wise cracking to break the tension at times but it's definitely not a comedy. The end of the world and the urgency of his mission is treated with all the dramatic weight it deserves. The first contact with an alien species is smartly handled and

actually uses real scientific methods to establish a common language. And the various challenges and disasters that threaten to derail their missions are very much presented as real dangers that are supposed to have the reader on edge. All of this is to say that if they can keep the tone mostly grounded in the reality of what's going on with a bit of levity thrown in at just the right times to break the tension, then I'm going to be a happy man. If on the other hand,

they try to turn it into the Lego movie in space, well then I'm going to have to stop being so polite. Ultimately though, what I've seen so far makes me cautiously optimistic for Project Hail Mary. I think and hope it's going to be good. And I really want to see inventive, thoughtprovoking sci-fi movies make a comeback because Jesus Christ, Hollywood needs to start taking on more original projects like this instead of churning out endless sequels to tired old franchises and beating the

dead horse of the superhero genre. Something has got to give. This film might just turn out to be Hollywood's own personal Hail Mary, too. And for once, I hope it works out for them. Anyway, that's all I've got for today. Go away now.
